134 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:長髄彦 up to rev 103146552
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2024年12月}} {{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 長髄彦 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = 上古 | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = [[神武天皇]]即位前 | 改名 = | 別名 = 登美能那賀須泥毘古、登美毘古、那賀須泥毘古、鳥見彦 等 | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[饒速日命]] | 氏族 = | 父母 = | 兄弟 = 妹：[[登美夜毘売]] | 妻 = | 子 = | 特記事項 = }} '''長髄彦'''（ながすねひこ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する伝承上の人物。[[神武天皇]]に抵抗した[[大和国|大和]]の指導者の一人。神武天皇との戦い（[[神武東征]]）に敗れた。
== 概要 == 『[[日本書紀]]』では'''長髄彦'''であるが、『[[古事記]]』では'''那賀須泥毘古'''、また'''登美能那賀須泥毘古'''（とみのながすねびこ）、'''登美毘古'''（とみびこ）とも表記される。[[神武東征]]の場面で、[[大和国|大和]]地方で東征に抵抗した[[豪族]]の長として描かれている人物。
『古事記』では特に討伐の場面もなく主君の[[ニギハヤヒ|邇芸速日命（にぎはやひのみこと）]]が神武天皇に服属したとするが、『日本書紀』では自己の正統性を主張するため互いに神璽を示し合ったが、それでも長髄彦が戦い続けたため饒速日命（邇芸速日命）の手によって殺されたとされる。
『[[先代旧事本紀]]』では神武天皇が紀伊半島を迂回し'''長髄彦'''と再び対峙した頃には、既に饒速日命は亡くなっており、[[ウマシマジ|宇摩志麻遅命（うましまぢのみこと）]]が天孫（神武天皇）への帰順を諭しても聞かなかったため殺したとする。
なお、長髄とは『日本書紀』では[[邑]]の名であるとされている。
== 神話での内容 == 登美夜毘売（とみやびめ）、あるいは[[ミカシキヤヒメ|三炊屋媛]]（みかしきやひめ）ともいう自らの妹を、天の磐舟で[[河内国]]の河上の哮ヶ峯（たけるがみね）に降臨し、その後[[大和国]]の鳥見の白庭山に移った[[ニギハヤヒ|饒速日命]]（にぎはやひのみこと）の妻とし、仕えるようになる。 <!-- この{{要出典範囲|子孫が[[聖徳太子]]と共に[[物部守屋]]を討った[[迹見赤檮]]（トミノイチイ）だとされている|date=2010年1月}}。-->
[[神武天皇]]が[[なにわ|浪速国]]青雲の[[枚方市|白肩津]]に到着したのち、孔舎衛坂（くさえのさか）で迎え撃ち、このときの戦いで天皇の兄の[[彦五瀬命]]は矢に当たって負傷し、後に死亡している。
その後、[[八十梟帥]]や[[兄磯城]]を討った皇軍と再び戦うことになる。このとき、[[金鵄|金色の鳶]]が飛んできて、神武天皇の弓弭に止まり、長髄彦の軍は眼が眩み、戦うことができなくなった。『日本書紀』神武紀には、この時の様子を次のように記している。 {{Quotation|{{ruby-ja|皇師|みいくさ}}遂に長髄彦を{{ruby-ja|撃|う}}つ。{{ruby-ja|連|しきり}}に戦ひて{{ruby-ja|取勝|か}}つこと{{ruby-ja|能|あた}}はず。時に{{ruby-ja|忽然|たちまち}}にして{{ruby-ja|天|ひ}}{{ruby-ja|陰|し}}けて{{ruby-ja|雨氷|ひさめ}}ふる。乃ち{{ruby-ja|金色|こがね}}の{{ruby-ja|霊|あや}}しき{{ruby-ja|鵄|とび}}有りて、飛び来りて{{ruby-ja|皇弓|みゆみ}}の{{ruby-ja|弭|はず}}に止れり。其の{{ruby-ja|鵄|とび}}{{ruby-ja|光|ひか}}り{{ruby-ja|曄煜|てりかかや}}きて、{{ruby-ja|状|かたち}}{{ruby-ja|流電|いなびかり}}の如し。是に由りて、長髄彦が{{ruby-ja|軍卒|いくさのひとども}}、皆迷ひ{{ruby-ja|眩|まぎ}}えて、{{ruby-ja|復|また}}{{ruby-ja|力|きは}}め戦はず。{{ruby-ja|長髄|ながすね}}は{{ruby-ja|是|これ}}{{ruby-ja|邑|むら}}の{{ruby-ja|本|もと}}の{{ruby-ja|號|な}}なり。因りて{{ruby-ja|亦|また}}以て人の名とす。{{ruby-ja|皇軍|みいくさ}}の、鵄の{{ruby-ja|瑞|みつ}}を得るに乃りて、{{ruby-ja|時人|ときのひと}}{{ruby-ja|仍|よ}}りて{{ruby-ja|鵄邑|とびのむら}}と{{ruby-ja|號|なづ}}く。今{{ruby-ja|鳥見|とみ}}と云ふは、{{ruby-ja|是|これ}}{{ruby-ja|訛|よこなば}}れるなり。|岩波日本古典文学大系}}
ここに長髄の名前が地名に由来すると記されているが、その一方で鳥見という地名が神武天皇の[[鳶]]に由来すると記されている。さてその後、長髄彦は神武天皇に「昔、天つ神の子が[[天磐船]]に乗って降臨した。名を[[ニギハヤヒ|櫛玉饒速日命]]という。私の妹の[[ミカシキヤヒメ|三炊屋媛]]を娶わせて、可美真手という子も生まれた。ゆえに私は饒速日命を君として仕えている。天つ神の子がどうして二人いようか。どうして天つ神の子であると称して人の土地を奪おうとしているのか」とその疑いを述べ、主君が天つ神の子である証拠として、[[天羽々矢|天の羽羽矢]]と步靫（かちゆき）を見せた。天皇が同じ物を見せると長髄彦は恐れ畏まったが、改心することはなかった。そのため、間を取り持つことが無理だと知った饒速日命に殺された。
== 中世の伝説 == 『[[曽我物語]]』等では[[安日彦]]（あびひこ）という兄弟がいるとされる。また中世の武将の[[安藤氏]]（後の[[子爵]][[秋田氏|秋田家]]）が長髄彦の子孫であると自称した。
== 一説 == 旧[[添下郡]]鳥見郷（現[[生駒市]]北部・[[奈良市]][[富雄駅|富雄]]地方）付近に勢力を持った豪族という説がある。[[生駒市]]白庭台の住宅地に長髄彦の本拠地があった場所とされる碑が建っている。
『古事記』、『日本書紀』、『先代旧事本紀』のいずれにも父祖の記載はなく、系譜は不明である。
[[添御県坐神社 (奈良市三碓町)|添御県坐神社]](奈良市三碓3丁目)八木尚宏宮司が平成21年に同神社宮司着任時に氏子総代会長より地元の伝承として、下記の事を伝えられた<ref>{{Citation|title=『長髄彦』宮司が語る衝撃の事実|last=神旅|date=2024-02-23|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PXa6QH0oYs4|access-date=2024-11-24}}</ref>(伊弉諾神社（生駒市上町）が上鳥見の、登彌神社（奈良市石木町）が下鳥見の、同社が中鳥見の、鎮守といわれている<ref>{{Cite web |title=添御縣坐神社本殿 {{!}} 奈良県歴史文化資源データベース {{!}} 奈良県歴史文化資源データベース「いかす・なら」 |url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/miryoku/ikasu-nara/bunkashigen/main10633.html |website=www.pref.nara.jp |access-date=2024-12-06}}</ref>)。
* 神社の本当の祭神は、長髄彦である。地域の支配者長髄彦は侵略者イワレビコ(神武天皇即位前)に敗れて自決し、地元民が御霊を祀る祠をつくり、これが神社のはじまりである。
* 明治維新を迎え、氏子が逆賊が祭神であることをはばかり学者に相談し、その助言により[[武乳速命|武乳速之命]](別名、[[天児屋命]]。春日神4柱の1柱)を祭神とすることにした。 『[[石切劔箭神社]]』の社史によれば、天照大神から大和建国の[[神勅]]を拝し『[[十種神宝|十種の瑞宝]]』を授かった饒速日尊が船団を組み、自らも『[[布都御魂|布都御魂劔]]』と日の御子の証である『[[天羽々矢]]』を携え[[磐船神社 (交野市)|天磐船]]に乗り込み、物部八十の大船団を率いて[[高天原]]を出航した。途中、[[豊前国]]の[[宇佐市|宇佐]]に寄港すると船団を二つに分け、息子の[[天香山命|天香具山命]]に『布都御魂劔』を授け船団の一方を預けた。宇佐から瀬戸内海を渡ると饒速日尊は河内・大和に、一方の天香具山命は紀伊に向かった。天磐船が鳥見の里を見渡す哮ヶ峯（たけるがみね『[[生駒山]]』）に着くと、饒速日尊は辺りを見渡し「'''虚空(そら)にみつ日本(やまと)国）'''」【訳「 空から見た日本の国」または「空に光り輝く日本の国」】と賛じた。これが[[日本]]の国号の始まりとなった。当時の河内と大和の一帯は鳥見の里と呼ばれ、穏やかな自然と海や山の幸に恵まれた豊な土地であった。この地方を治めていた豪族、鳥見一族は、稲作や製鉄の技術がないものの、狩や漁がうまく、生活用具や住居づくりに優れ、長身の恵まれた体格は戦闘に秀で「長髄の者」と恐れられていた。その頃の鳥見一族の長、'''長髄彦'''は饒速日尊の徳の高さに打たれ、尊のもたらした稲作や織物、製鉄の道具・武具に文化の差をみると、争う事の無益さを悟り、一族こぞって饒速日尊に従った。この時二人の間を取り持ったのが'''長髄彦'''の妹、[[ミカシキヤヒメ|登美夜毘売（三炊屋媛）]]で後に尊との間に[[ウマシマジ|宇摩志麻遅命]]（うましまぢのみこと）をもうけた{{要出典|date=2024年12月}}。
こうして鳥見の里を治めるようになった饒速日尊は、水が豊かで稲作に適したこの土地に水田を拓き、大きな実りをもらすようになった。これが近畿地方の稲作文化の初めとなった。一方、鳥見の里が繁栄をきわめていた頃、磐余彦（後の神武天皇）が[[日向国|日向]]の[[高千穂]]から東へ進行を続け（[[神武東征]]）、やがて河内に上陸し孔舎衙坂で'''長髄彦'''と対峙した。戦いに敗れた磐余彦は紀伊方面に退却、紀伊半島を迂回し再び'''長髄彦'''と対峙する。この頃、既に饒速日尊は亡くなり、代わって鳥見の長となっていたのは宇摩志麻遅命だった。宇摩志麻遅命は「天羽々矢」と[[箙|歩靭]]（かちゆき）を、日の御子である証として磐余彦に差し出した。すると磐余彦からも同じものが示され天孫であることが明らかになった。宇摩志麻遅命は'''長髄彦'''に磐余彦への帰順をさとし自らも一族を率いて磐余彦に忠誠を誓い、広大な稲作地や所領のすべてと天照大神から授かった『[[十種神宝|十種の瑞宝]]』を磐余彦に捧げた。こうして大和の統一が成し遂げられ磐余彦は始馭天下之天皇（神武天皇）に即位した。
== 脚註 == {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[長髄彦]] to [[Nagatsuhiko]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2024年12月}} {{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 長髄彦 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = 上古 | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = [[神武天皇]]即位前 | 改名 = | 別名 = 登美能那賀須泥毘古、登美毘古、那賀須泥毘古、鳥見彦 等 | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[饒速日命]] | 氏族 = | 父母 = | 兄弟 = 妹：[[登美夜毘売]] | 妻 = | 子 = | 特記事項 = }} '''長髄彦'''（ながすねひこ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する伝承上の人物。[[神武天皇]]に抵抗した[[大和国|大和]]の指導者の一人。神武天皇との戦い（[[神武東征]]）に敗れた。
== 概要 == 『[[日本書紀]]』では'''長髄彦'''であるが、『[[古事記]]』では'''那賀須泥毘古'''、また'''登美能那賀須泥毘古'''（とみのながすねびこ）、'''登美毘古'''（とみびこ）とも表記される。[[神武東征]]の場面で、[[大和国|大和]]地方で東征に抵抗した[[豪族]]の長として描かれている人物。
『古事記』では特に討伐の場面もなく主君の[[ニギハヤヒ|邇芸速日命（にぎはやひのみこと）]]が神武天皇に服属したとするが、『日本書紀』では自己の正統性を主張するため互いに神璽を示し合ったが、それでも長髄彦が戦い続けたため饒速日命（邇芸速日命）の手によって殺されたとされる。
『[[先代旧事本紀]]』では神武天皇が紀伊半島を迂回し'''長髄彦'''と再び対峙した頃には、既に饒速日命は亡くなっており、[[ウマシマジ|宇摩志麻遅命（うましまぢのみこと）]]が天孫（神武天皇）への帰順を諭しても聞かなかったため殺したとする。
なお、長髄とは『日本書紀』では[[邑]]の名であるとされている。
== 神話での内容 == 登美夜毘売（とみやびめ）、あるいは[[ミカシキヤヒメ|三炊屋媛]]（みかしきやひめ）ともいう自らの妹を、天の磐舟で[[河内国]]の河上の哮ヶ峯（たけるがみね）に降臨し、その後[[大和国]]の鳥見の白庭山に移った[[ニギハヤヒ|饒速日命]]（にぎはやひのみこと）の妻とし、仕えるようになる。 <!-- この{{要出典範囲|子孫が[[聖徳太子]]と共に[[物部守屋]]を討った[[迹見赤檮]]（トミノイチイ）だとされている|date=2010年1月}}。-->
[[神武天皇]]が[[なにわ|浪速国]]青雲の[[枚方市|白肩津]]に到着したのち、孔舎衛坂（くさえのさか）で迎え撃ち、このときの戦いで天皇の兄の[[彦五瀬命]]は矢に当たって負傷し、後に死亡している。
その後、[[八十梟帥]]や[[兄磯城]]を討った皇軍と再び戦うことになる。このとき、[[金鵄|金色の鳶]]が飛んできて、神武天皇の弓弭に止まり、長髄彦の軍は眼が眩み、戦うことができなくなった。『日本書紀』神武紀には、この時の様子を次のように記している。 {{Quotation|{{ruby-ja|皇師|みいくさ}}遂に長髄彦を{{ruby-ja|撃|う}}つ。{{ruby-ja|連|しきり}}に戦ひて{{ruby-ja|取勝|か}}つこと{{ruby-ja|能|あた}}はず。時に{{ruby-ja|忽然|たちまち}}にして{{ruby-ja|天|ひ}}{{ruby-ja|陰|し}}けて{{ruby-ja|雨氷|ひさめ}}ふる。乃ち{{ruby-ja|金色|こがね}}の{{ruby-ja|霊|あや}}しき{{ruby-ja|鵄|とび}}有りて、飛び来りて{{ruby-ja|皇弓|みゆみ}}の{{ruby-ja|弭|はず}}に止れり。其の{{ruby-ja|鵄|とび}}{{ruby-ja|光|ひか}}り{{ruby-ja|曄煜|てりかかや}}きて、{{ruby-ja|状|かたち}}{{ruby-ja|流電|いなびかり}}の如し。是に由りて、長髄彦が{{ruby-ja|軍卒|いくさのひとども}}、皆迷ひ{{ruby-ja|眩|まぎ}}えて、{{ruby-ja|復|また}}{{ruby-ja|力|きは}}め戦はず。{{ruby-ja|長髄|ながすね}}は{{ruby-ja|是|これ}}{{ruby-ja|邑|むら}}の{{ruby-ja|本|もと}}の{{ruby-ja|號|な}}なり。因りて{{ruby-ja|亦|また}}以て人の名とす。{{ruby-ja|皇軍|みいくさ}}の、鵄の{{ruby-ja|瑞|みつ}}を得るに乃りて、{{ruby-ja|時人|ときのひと}}{{ruby-ja|仍|よ}}りて{{ruby-ja|鵄邑|とびのむら}}と{{ruby-ja|號|なづ}}く。今{{ruby-ja|鳥見|とみ}}と云ふは、{{ruby-ja|是|これ}}{{ruby-ja|訛|よこなば}}れるなり。|岩波日本古典文学大系}}
ここに長髄の名前が地名に由来すると記されているが、その一方で鳥見という地名が神武天皇の[[鳶]]に由来すると記されている。さてその後、長髄彦は神武天皇に「昔、天つ神の子が[[天磐船]]に乗って降臨した。名を[[ニギハヤヒ|櫛玉饒速日命]]という。私の妹の[[ミカシキヤヒメ|三炊屋媛]]を娶わせて、可美真手という子も生まれた。ゆえに私は饒速日命を君として仕えている。天つ神の子がどうして二人いようか。どうして天つ神の子であると称して人の土地を奪おうとしているのか」とその疑いを述べ、主君が天つ神の子である証拠として、[[天羽々矢|天の羽羽矢]]と步靫（かちゆき）を見せた。天皇が同じ物を見せると長髄彦は恐れ畏まったが、改心することはなかった。そのため、間を取り持つことが無理だと知った饒速日命に殺された。
== 中世の伝説 == 『[[曽我物語]]』等では[[安日彦]]（あびひこ）という兄弟がいるとされる。また中世の武将の[[安藤氏]]（後の[[子爵]][[秋田氏|秋田家]]）が長髄彦の子孫であると自称した。
== 一説 == 旧[[添下郡]]鳥見郷（現[[生駒市]]北部・[[奈良市]][[富雄駅|富雄]]地方）付近に勢力を持った豪族という説がある。[[生駒市]]白庭台の住宅地に長髄彦の本拠地があった場所とされる碑が建っている。
『古事記』、『日本書紀』、『先代旧事本紀』のいずれにも父祖の記載はなく、系譜は不明である。
[[添御県坐神社 (奈良市三碓町)|添御県坐神社]](奈良市三碓3丁目)八木尚宏宮司が平成21年に同神社宮司着任時に氏子総代会長より地元の伝承として、下記の事を伝えられた<ref>{{Citation|title=『長髄彦』宮司が語る衝撃の事実|last=神旅|date=2024-02-23|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PXa6QH0oYs4|access-date=2024-11-24}}</ref>(伊弉諾神社（生駒市上町）が上鳥見の、登彌神社（奈良市石木町）が下鳥見の、同社が中鳥見の、鎮守といわれている<ref>{{Cite web |title=添御縣坐神社本殿 {{!}} 奈良県歴史文化資源データベース {{!}} 奈良県歴史文化資源データベース「いかす・なら」 |url=https://www.pref.nara.jp/miryoku/ikasu-nara/bunkashigen/main10633.html |website=www.pref.nara.jp |access-date=2024-12-06}}</ref>)。
* 神社の本当の祭神は、長髄彦である。地域の支配者長髄彦は侵略者イワレビコ(神武天皇即位前)に敗れて自決し、地元民が御霊を祀る祠をつくり、これが神社のはじまりである。
* 明治維新を迎え、氏子が逆賊が祭神であることをはばかり学者に相談し、その助言により[[武乳速命|武乳速之命]](別名、[[天児屋命]]。春日神4柱の1柱)を祭神とすることにした。 『[[石切劔箭神社]]』の社史によれば、天照大神から大和建国の[[神勅]]を拝し『[[十種神宝|十種の瑞宝]]』を授かった饒速日尊が船団を組み、自らも『[[布都御魂|布都御魂劔]]』と日の御子の証である『[[天羽々矢]]』を携え[[磐船神社 (交野市)|天磐船]]に乗り込み、物部八十の大船団を率いて[[高天原]]を出航した。途中、[[豊前国]]の[[宇佐市|宇佐]]に寄港すると船団を二つに分け、息子の[[天香山命|天香具山命]]に『布都御魂劔』を授け船団の一方を預けた。宇佐から瀬戸内海を渡ると饒速日尊は河内・大和に、一方の天香具山命は紀伊に向かった。天磐船が鳥見の里を見渡す哮ヶ峯（たけるがみね『[[生駒山]]』）に着くと、饒速日尊は辺りを見渡し「'''虚空(そら)にみつ日本(やまと)国）'''」【訳「 空から見た日本の国」または「空に光り輝く日本の国」】と賛じた。これが[[日本]]の国号の始まりとなった。当時の河内と大和の一帯は鳥見の里と呼ばれ、穏やかな自然と海や山の幸に恵まれた豊な土地であった。この地方を治めていた豪族、鳥見一族は、稲作や製鉄の技術がないものの、狩や漁がうまく、生活用具や住居づくりに優れ、長身の恵まれた体格は戦闘に秀で「長髄の者」と恐れられていた。その頃の鳥見一族の長、'''長髄彦'''は饒速日尊の徳の高さに打たれ、尊のもたらした稲作や織物、製鉄の道具・武具に文化の差をみると、争う事の無益さを悟り、一族こぞって饒速日尊に従った。この時二人の間を取り持ったのが'''長髄彦'''の妹、[[ミカシキヤヒメ|登美夜毘売（三炊屋媛）]]で後に尊との間に[[ウマシマジ|宇摩志麻遅命]]（うましまぢのみこと）をもうけた{{要出典|date=2024年12月}}。
こうして鳥見の里を治めるようになった饒速日尊は、水が豊かで稲作に適したこの土地に水田を拓き、大きな実りをもらすようになった。これが近畿地方の稲作文化の初めとなった。一方、鳥見の里が繁栄をきわめていた頃、磐余彦（後の神武天皇）が[[日向国|日向]]の[[高千穂]]から東へ進行を続け（[[神武東征]]）、やがて河内に上陸し孔舎衙坂で'''長髄彦'''と対峙した。戦いに敗れた磐余彦は紀伊方面に退却、紀伊半島を迂回し再び'''長髄彦'''と対峙する。この頃、既に饒速日尊は亡くなり、代わって鳥見の長となっていたのは宇摩志麻遅命だった。宇摩志麻遅命は「天羽々矢」と[[箙|歩靭]]（かちゆき）を、日の御子である証として磐余彦に差し出した。すると磐余彦からも同じものが示され天孫であることが明らかになった。宇摩志麻遅命は'''長髄彦'''に磐余彦への帰順をさとし自らも一族を率いて磐余彦に忠誠を誓い、広大な稲作地や所領のすべてと天照大神から授かった『[[十種神宝|十種の瑞宝]]』を磐余彦に捧げた。こうして大和の統一が成し遂げられ磐余彦は始馭天下之天皇（神武天皇）に即位した。

{{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[Yamato Province]] who opposed [[Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}, while the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft}} refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the {{ill|Sendai Kujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft}}, Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, {{ill|Hikoiwase|ja|彦五瀬命|qq=draft}}, was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the {{ill|Soga Monogatari|ja|曽我物語|qq=draft}} mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft}}. The [[Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft}} (modern {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市|qq=draft}}). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[Yamato Province]] who opposed [[Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, while the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|qq=}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the {{ill|Sendai Kujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Kujihongi|qq=}}, Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, {{ill|Hikoiwase|ja|彦五瀬命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwase|qq=}}, was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the {{ill|Soga Monogatari|ja|曽我物語|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga Monogatari|qq=}} mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=}}. The [[Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=}} (modern {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikoma|qq=}} and {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of people in ancient Japan|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of people in ancient Japan|qq=}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe clan|qq=}} * {{ill|Miwa clan|ja|三輪氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=}} * {{ill|Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|ja|アシナヅチ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinazuchi|qq=}}

== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, {{ill|Hikoiwase|ja|彦五瀬命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoiwase|qq=}}, was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the {{ill|Soga Monogatari|ja|曽我物語|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga Monogatari|qq=}} mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=}}. The [[Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=}} (modern {{ill|Ikoma|ja|生駒市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikoma|qq=}} and {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

{{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[Yamato Province]] who opposed [[Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|qq=}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=}}. The [[Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[Yamato Province]] who opposed [[Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|qq=}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=}}. The [[Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of people in ancient Japan|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of people in ancient Japan|qq=}} * [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] * {{ill|Miwa clan|ja|三輪氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=}} * {{ill|Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|ja|アシナヅチ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinazuchi|qq=}}
{{Shinto-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|長髄彦|version=103146552|comment=Imported full ja history}}

== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

<!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--> [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:大和国の人物]] [[Category:奈良市の歴史]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:生駒市の歴史]] [[Category:紀元前663年没]]

{{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[Yamato Province]] who opposed [[Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|qq=}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=}}. The [[Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=}}. The [[Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

{{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[Yamato Province]] who opposed [[Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|qq=}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.

Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=}}. <!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--> [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Yamato Province]] [[Category:History of Nara]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:生駒市の歴史]] [[Category:紀元前663年没]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:나가스네히코]] [[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]] [[zh:長髓彥]]

{{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[Yamato Province]] who opposed [[Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=}}. The [[Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of people in ancient Japan|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of people in ancient Japan|qq=}} * [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] * {{ill|Miwa clan|ja|三輪氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神氏}} * {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}} * {{ill|Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|ja|アシナヅチ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinazuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アシナヅチ・テナヅチ}}

The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

{{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[Yamato Province]] who opposed [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[Yamato Province]] who opposed [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[:en:Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of people in ancient Japan|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of people in ancient Japan|qq=}} * [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] * {{ill|Miwa clan|ja|三輪氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神氏}} * {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}} * {{ill|Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|ja|アシナヅチ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinazuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アシナヅチ・テナヅチ}}

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:生駒の Municipal History]] into [[Category:History of Ikoma City]] via bot
revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] who opposed [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[:en:Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of people in ancient Japan|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of people in ancient Japan|qq=}} * [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] * {{ill|Miwa clan|ja|三輪氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神氏}} * {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}} * {{ill|Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|ja|アシナヅチ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinazuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アシナヅチ・テナヅチ}}
{{Shinto-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}}

{{Infobox person|death_date=0663|death_place={{ill|Nara Basin|qid=Q10942269}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|politician|qid=Q82955}}|father={{ill|Kotoshironushi|qid=Q8009611}}|relatives={{ill|Mikashikiyahime|qid=Q11341880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q4922278]] Wikidata name: Nagasunehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] who opposed [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[:en:Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.
