[[ja:長髄彦]]
<!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--> [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Yamato Province]] [[Category:History of Nara]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:History of Ikoma City]] [[Category:Deaths in 663 BC]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:나가스네히코]] [[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]] [[zh:長髓彥]] {{wikidata link|Q4922278}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] {{Infobox person|death_date=0663|death_place={{ill|Nara Basin|qid=Q10942269}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|politician|qid=Q82955}}|father={{ill|Kotoshironushi|qid=Q8009611}}|relatives={{ill|Mikashikiyahime|qid=Q11341880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q4922278]] Wikidata name: Nagasunehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] who opposed [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[:en:Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|qq=|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|qq=|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|qq=|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|qq=|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

<!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--> [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Yamato Province]] [[Category:History of Nara]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:History of Ikoma City]] [[Category:Deaths in 663 BC]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:나가스네히코]] [[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]] [[zh:長髓彥]] {{wikidata link|Q4922278}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] {{Infobox person|death_date=0663|death_place={{ill|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}|father={{ill|Kotoshironushi|WD=Q8009611}}|relatives={{ill|Mikashikiyahime|WD=Q11341880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4922278]] Wikidata name: Nagasunehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] who opposed [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[:en:Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[:en:Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] {{Infobox person|death_date=0663|death_place={{ill|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}|father={{ill|Kotoshironushi|WD=Q8009611}}|relatives={{ill|Mikashikiyahime|WD=Q11341880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4922278]] Wikidata name: Nagasunehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] who opposed [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[:en:Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[:en:Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] {{Infobox person|death_date=0663|death_place={{ill|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}|father={{ill|Kotoshironushi|WD=Q8009611}}|relatives={{ill|Mikashikiyahime|WD=Q11341880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4922278]] Wikidata name: Nagasunehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] who opposed [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[:en:Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of people in ancient Japan|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of people in ancient Japan|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}} * [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] * {{ill|Miwa clan|ja|三輪氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神氏}} * {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}} * {{ill|Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|ja|アシナヅチ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アシナヅチ・テナヅチ}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] {{Infobox person|death_date=0663|death_place={{ill|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}|father={{ill|Kotoshironushi|WD=Q8009611}}|relatives={{ill|Mikashikiyahime|WD=Q11341880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4922278]] Wikidata name: Nagasunehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] who opposed [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[:en:Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] {{Infobox person|death_date=0663|death_place={{ill|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}|father={{ill|Kotoshironushi|WD=Q8009611}}|relatives={{ill|Mikashikiyahime|WD=Q11341880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4922278]] Wikidata name: Nagasunehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}

After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] {{Infobox person|death_date=0663|death_place={{ill|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}|father={{ill|Kotoshironushi|WD=Q8009611}}|relatives={{ill|Mikashikiyahime|WD=Q11341880}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4922278]] Wikidata name: Nagasunehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] who opposed [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], while the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kujihongi]], Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]] aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the [[:en:Hirakata|White Shoulder Port]] of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, [[:en:Itsuse no Mikoto|Hikoiwase]], was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the [[:en:Soga Monogatari|Soga Monogatari]] mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The [[:en:Andō clan|Andō clan]], later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern [[:en:Ikoma, Nara|Ikoma]] and [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, while the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kujihongi}}, Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}} aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the {{ill|Hirakata|en|Hirakata|WD=Q270912|lt=White Shoulder Port}} of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, {{ill|Itsuse no Mikoto|en|Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970|lt=Hikoiwase}}, was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the {{ill|Soga Monogatari|en|Soga Monogatari|WD=Q217886|lt=Soga Monogatari}} mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The {{ill|Andō clan|en|Andō clan|WD=Q2975213|lt=Andō clan}}, later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern {{ill|Ikoma, Nara|en|Ikoma, Nara|WD=Q328610|lt=Ikoma}} and {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} who opposed {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}} during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.

In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.

The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] {{Infobox person|death_date=0663|death_place={{ill|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}|father={{ill|Kotoshironushi|WD=Q8009611}}|relatives={{ill|Mikashikiyahime|WD=Q11341880}}}} {{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}} | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} who opposed {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}} during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, while the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kujihongi}}, Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}} aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the {{ill|Hirakata|en|Hirakata|WD=Q270912|lt=White Shoulder Port}} of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, {{ill|Itsuse no Mikoto|en|Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970|lt=Hikoiwase}}, was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the {{ill|Soga Monogatari|en|Soga Monogatari|WD=Q217886|lt=Soga Monogatari}} mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The {{ill|Andō clan|en|Andō clan|WD=Q2975213|lt=Andō clan}}, later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern {{ill|Ikoma, Nara|en|Ikoma, Nara|WD=Q328610|lt=Ikoma}} and {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of people in ancient Japan|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of people in ancient Japan|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan}} * {{ill|Miwa clan|ja|三輪氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神氏}} * {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}} * {{ill|Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|ja|アシナヅチ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アシナヅチ・テナヅチ}}

She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the {{ill|Hirakata|en|Hirakata|WD=Q270912|lt=White Shoulder Port}} of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, {{ill|Itsuse no Mikoto|en|Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970|lt=Hikoiwase}}, was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the {{ill|Soga Monogatari|en|Soga Monogatari|WD=Q217886|lt=Soga Monogatari}} mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The {{ill|Andō clan|en|Andō clan|WD=Q2975213|lt=Andō clan}}, later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern {{ill|Ikoma, Nara|en|Ikoma, Nara|WD=Q328610|lt=Ikoma}} and {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} who opposed {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, while the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kujihongi|1=Kujiki}}, Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}} aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the {{ill|Hirakata|en|Hirakata|WD=Q270912|lt=White Shoulder Port|1=Hirakata}} of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, {{ill|Itsuse no Mikoto|en|Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970|lt=Hikoiwase|1=Itsuse no Mikoto}}, was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the {{ill|Soga Monogatari|en|Soga Monogatari|WD=Q217886|lt=Soga Monogatari|1=Soga Monogatari}} mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The {{ill|Andō clan|en|Andō clan|WD=Q2975213|lt=Andō clan|1=Andō clan}}, later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern {{ill|Ikoma, Nara|en|Ikoma, Nara|WD=Q328610|lt=Ikoma|1=Ikoma, Nara}} and {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Q45075623|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

<!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--><!--Nagatsuhiko--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:나가스네히코]] [[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]] [[zh:長髓彥]] {{wikidata link|Q4922278}} [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Deaths in 663 BC]] [[Category:History of Ikoma City]] [[Category:History of Nara]] [[Category:Japanese monarchs]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:長髄彦]][[ko:나가스네히코]][[th:นางาซูเนะ โนะ ฮิโกะ]][[zh:長髓彥]] {{Infobox person|death_date=0663|death_place={{ill|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|1=Nara Basin}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|occupation={{ill|politician|WD=Q82955|1=Politician}}|father={{ill|Kotoshironushi|WD=Q8009611|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}|relatives={{ill|Mikashikiyahime|WD=Q11341880|1=Mikashikiya-hime}}}} {{Unreferenced|date=December 2024}} {{Infobox noble | name = Nagasunehiko | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = Ancient | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Before the enthronement of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} | alias = Tomino Nagasunehiko, Tomibiko, Nagasunehiko, Torimihiko | titles = | deification = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Nigihayahi no Mikoto]] | clan = | parents = | siblings = Sister: {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | spouse = | children = | notable_facts = Resisted Emperor Jimmu during the [[Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] }}
'''Nagasunehiko''' (長髄彦) is a legendary figure from [[Japanese mythology]]. He is known as one of the leaders of {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} who opposed {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} during the [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Eastern Expedition of Emperor Jimmu]] but was ultimately defeated.
== Overview == The name '''Nagasunehiko''' appears in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, while the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} refers to him as '''Tomino Nagasunehiko''' (登美能那賀須泥毘古) or '''Tomibiko''' (登美毘古). He is described as a leader of the [[gōzoku]] (regional clans) in the Yamato region who resisted Emperor Jimmu's forces.
According to the Kojiki, Nagasunehiko did not resist Emperor Jimmu and instead pledged allegiance when his lord, {{ill|Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, submitted to Jimmu. In contrast, the Nihon Shoki depicts him continuing to fight even after verifying his lord's divine authority, ultimately leading to his death at Nigihayahi's hands.
In the {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kujihongi|1=Kujiki}}, Emperor Jimmu confronts Nagasunehiko after detouring around the [[Kii Peninsula]], at which point Nigihayahi is already deceased. Nagasunehiko refuses to submit despite admonishment by {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}} and is killed.
The Nihon Shoki mentions that "Nagasune" was originally a place name.
== Mythological Account == Nagasunehiko's sister, {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|ja|三炊屋媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikashikiyahime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, became the wife of Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had descended from the heavens at Mount Takeru in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}} aboard the [[Ame-no-Iwafune|Heavenly Rock Ship]]. She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the {{ill|Hirakata|en|Hirakata|WD=Q270912|lt=White Shoulder Port|1=Hirakata}} of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, {{ill|Itsuse no Mikoto|en|Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970|lt=Hikoiwase|1=Itsuse no Mikoto}}, was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the {{ill|Soga Monogatari|en|Soga Monogatari|WD=Q217886|lt=Soga Monogatari|1=Soga Monogatari}} mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10946871}}. The {{ill|Andō clan|en|Andō clan|WD=Q2975213|lt=Andō clan|1=Andō clan}}, later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175345}} (modern {{ill|Ikoma, Nara|en|Ikoma, Nara|WD=Q328610|lt=Ikoma|1=Ikoma, Nara}} and {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Q45075623|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.

She later bore him {{ill|Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji|lt=Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja|宇摩志麻遅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umashimaji no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウマシマジ}}.
When Emperor Jimmu reached the {{ill|Hirakata|en|Hirakata|qid=Q270912|lt=White Shoulder Port|1=Hirakata}} of [[Naniwa Province]], he was confronted by Nagasunehiko at Kusakae Hill. During this battle, Jimmu's brother, {{ill|Itsuse no Mikoto|en|Itsuse no Mikoto|qid=Q10881970|lt=Hikoiwase|1=Itsuse no Mikoto}}, was wounded by an arrow and later died.
Subsequently, Jimmu's forces defeated other local leaders, including the {{ill|Yasotakeru|lt=Yasotakeru|lt=Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Bandit Chiefs|qq=}} and {{ill|Aniiso|ja|兄磯城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aniiso|1=Eshiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q55530285}}. Nagasunehiko's forces were finally defeated when a divine golden kite perched on Jimmu's bowstring, dazzling and disorienting the enemy.
In the Nihon Shoki, Nagasunehiko questioned Jimmu's claim to divine descent, citing his allegiance to Nigihayahi no Mikoto, who had married his sister and borne a son. After comparing divine tokens such as the {{ill|Heavenly Feathered Arrow|ja|天羽々矢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Feathered Arrow|1=Ame no Habaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10940644}} and confirming Jimmu's legitimacy, Nagasunehiko refused to submit and was slain by Nigihayahi.
== Medieval Legends == Medieval texts like the {{ill|Soga Monogatari|en|Soga Monogatari|qid=Q217886|lt=Soga Monogatari|1=Soga Monogatari}} mention Nagasunehiko's brother, {{ill|Abihiko|ja|安日彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abihiko|1=Abihiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10946871}}. The {{ill|Andō clan|en|Andō clan|qid=Q2975213|lt=Andō clan|1=Andō clan}}, later the {{ill|Akita family|ja|秋田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 秋田氏|1=Akita clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2975198}}, claimed descent from Nagasunehiko.
== Modern Interpretations == Some theories suggest Nagasunehiko was a local leader in the {{ill|Tomi region|ja|鳥見郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomi region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富雄町|1=Tomio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17175345}} (modern {{ill|Ikoma, Nara|en|Ikoma, Nara|qid=Q328610|lt=Ikoma|1=Ikoma, Nara}} and {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}). A monument in {{ill|Shiraniwadai|ja|白庭台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraniwadai|1=Shiraniwadai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11579768}}, Ikoma, marks the site traditionally associated with his stronghold.
Other interpretations propose that Nagasunehiko's resistance represents a symbolic struggle between indigenous and imperial forces during the Yamato state's consolidation.
== Shrines and Legacy == Local legends and shrines such as the {{ill|Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja|添御縣坐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soe Mie Kenza Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 添御県坐神社|1=Q45075623|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q45075623}} are believed to honor Nagasunehiko. These sites commemorate his role in local history and mythology.
